traces.prompt:
You have just internalized a founder's voice. Now verify your calibration before writing any of the 9 posts per source.

If this check fails, the voice read is wrong — don't start writing. Go back and re-map voice markers. The problem is upstream.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS
- Opens with a paradoxical declarative statement — no preamble, no 'I've been thinking about...' Just the claim. ('Everyone is wrong about which incumbents AI will kill.' / 'The worst operators I know can name exactly who the CEO trusts.')
- Uses 'you're not X, you're Y' reframe structure — ('You're not cutting costs, you're cutting the pipeline that produces your next decade of leadership.' / 'You're not getting better at serving the market, you're getting better at serving the people who already fit.')
- Second-person 'you' as implication device — makes the reader feel they might be the one making the mistake, without accusation
- Em dashes for mid-sentence pivots and parenthetical precision — used heavily, 2-4 per post, never semicolons
- Closing with a conditional reframe: 'If your [X] isn't [Y], you're [Z]' — ('If your retention work isn't changing who you acquire, you're optimizing the wrong layer.' / 'If your trust surface is under 10 people in a company over 200, you're not building an organization. You're building a waiting room.')
- Never says 'AI is replacing humans' — always reframes as 'AI exposes who was actually adding value'
- Uses 'quietly' as a signal word for hidden damage — ('quietly defunded three years ago,' 'quietly blow up a company')
- Defines by negation — tells you what something is NOT before telling you what it IS ('High retention doesn't mean you built something great, it means...')
- Uses 'compounds' and compounding language as a recurring evaluative frame — trust compounds, intensity compounds, waiting compounds, antibodies compound
- Ends posts with a period, never a question. Never asks 'What do you think?' or 'Agree?' — the closing is always a statement that lands like a verdict

## WORD COUNT RANGE
165-310 words

## FORMATTING HABITS
{'uses_bullets': False, 'uses_emojis': False, 'uses_headers': False, 'em_dashes': True, 'lowercase_style': False, 'paragraph_style': 'short — typically 1-3 sentences per paragraph, rarely more than 4', 'line_break_frequency': 'Double line break between every paragraph. Each thought gets its own block. No dense walls of text. Typical post has 6-10 paragraph blocks.', 'additional_notes': "No hashtags. No tagging other people. No 'link in comments.' No bullet points or numbered lists. No emojis ever. No headers or bold text. Occasional use of quotes from agents or investors set off in their own paragraph. Posts begin immediately with the argument — no greeting, no 'I've been reflecting on...' Paragraphs breathe. White space is structural, not decorative. Commas used for rhythm and pacing within sentences. Occasional use of a very short standalone sentence (3-6 words) for emphasis after a longer explanatory paragraph."}

## ARGUMENT RHYTHM
Sharath's argument rhythm follows a consistent 5-beat pattern:

1. PARADOXICAL OPENING — He opens with a declarative statement that inverts conventional wisdom. It reads as counterintuitive or even wrong on first pass. ('Startups treat retention like a success metric when it's actually a signal of constraint.' / 'The worst operators I know can name exactly who the CEO trusts.' / 'Most founder advice optimizes for not failing instead of winning.')

2. REFRAME THE FRAME — He doesn't argue against the conventional view directly. He reframes WHY the conventional view exists — structural incentives, LP storytelling needs, survivorship bias, media cycles. This is diagnosis, not disagreement. ('It comes from pattern recognition, which is useful, but pattern recognition smooths for the median.')

3. MECHANISM EXPOSITION — He explains the hidden mechanics of how the wrong frame produces wrong outcomes. This is always concrete and causal, never abstract. He uses second-person 'you' to make the reader feel implicated. ('You start building for the retained cohort, tightening the product around their use case, making the experience stickier for people who were already staying.')

4. COMPOUNDING CONSEQUENCE — He shows how the wrong frame compounds over time, getting worse not better. The word 'compounds' or the concept of compounding appears frequently. ('This compounds. The people outside the trust surface get better at waiting, not deciding.')

5. CLOSING REFRAME / DIRECTIVE — He ends with either a restatement of the paradox now proven, or a single directive sentence that reframes the reader's next action. Never a call to action. Never 'what do you think?' Always a statement. ('If your retention work isn't changing who you acquire, you're optimizing the wrong layer.' / 'The entry-level role was never just a cost center. It was always your R&D lab for people.')

He toggles between universal principle and specific example within each beat, but the overall arc is always: invert → diagnose → mechanize → compound → reframe. He does NOT spiral. He escalates linearly with increasing stakes. Personal stories appear in beats 1-2 as credibility anchors, not in the closing.

## SAMPLE OF THEIR ACTUAL POSTS (read these carefully — this is the ground truth)
﻿Post
	Number of likes
	Number of comments
	Reposts
	At 19, I was moonlighting at a call center while studying in Bangalore, India. But that entry level job wasn't just income, it was my first real discovery of the problem I'd spend the next 20 years solving, and building two unicorns around.


That 19-year-old kid is the reason a million agents have Sanas running on their desktops today.


So, when I hear companies telling me that they're replacing their entry-level roles with AI, I can't help but wonder if this is the most profitable form of corporate suicide right now.


Think about what you're actually saying.


Entry-level developers become senior engineers. Entry-level salespeople become CROs. Junior analysts become the people who know where the bodies are buried and how to build the business. You're not cutting costs, you're cutting the pipeline that produces your next decade of leadership.


History is not kind to industries or even civilizations who stopped training their next batch of leaders. Manufacturing did the same with apprenticeships. Germany didn't. Look at who still makes things that matter.


The contact center industry is living this right now. Every "agent-less" narrative has accelerated offshoring, not reduced headcount. Because someone still has to handle the call the AI couldn't close. And that someone is increasingly untrained, because the pipeline that once built them was quietly defunded three years ago.


There's only so much talent that walks in the door already formed. Most of it gets made on the floor, in the rejection, in the muscle memory of doing the hard, unglamorous work at the bottom.


When you automate away the bottom, you don't get a leaner company. You get a company with a missing generation.


The entry-level role was never just a cost center. It was always your R&D lab for people.
	163
	10
	0
	Everyone is wrong about which incumbents AI will kill.


The conventional wisdom: the companies everyone writes off as too slow to adapt are AI's primary victims. Their scale, their legacy, their resistance to change make them most exposed.


That logic ignores what actually makes a business defensible.


Technology is roughly 5% of most companies' moat. The other 95% accumulates - proprietary data from millions of transactions, distribution relationships that took decades to build, regulatory positioning competitors can't fast-follow, operational integration so deep that replication would cost years and hundreds of millions.


AI replicates the 5%. It doesn't touch the 95%.


So disruption isn't uniform. Simpler companies - those whose defensibility lives in software elegance or cost arbitrage - are genuinely threatened. Their moat is exactly what AI commoditizes. But complex companies - those whose defensibility lives below the software layer - don't get disrupted. They get amplified. AI hands them analytical capability without giving any attacker access to the structural conditions that made them defensible in 

---

## TASK

Draft ONE opening paragraph (2-3 sentences) on this neutral topic: "What most people get wrong about hiring."

This is a calibration test. The paragraph must:
1. Sound like THIS specific founder wrote it — not "a CEO" or "a thought leader" or "a generic LinkedIn poster"
2. Match their sentence rhythm, word choices, and formatting habits EXACTLY
3. Fall within their typical word count density
4. Use at least 2 of their voice markers naturally
5. Match their formatting: if they use em dashes, use em dashes. If they tag team members, end with tags. If they use ":)", use ":)". Formatting mismatch is the biggest AI tell.
6. Avoid every pattern on the anti-slop list:
   - No "In today's..."
   - No "Here's the thing"
   - No "Hot take:" / "Unpopular opinion:"
   - No "Let that sink in" / "Read that again"
   - No "Game-changer" / "Landscape" / "Navigate" / "Leverage"

The recognition test: would this founder read the paragraph and think "this is what I was going to say"? If they'd have to rewrite more than one sentence, the voice work failed.

## OUTPUT FORMAT

Return a JSON object:
```json
{{
  "calibration_paragraph": "the opening paragraph you wrote",
  "voice_markers_used": ["which specific markers from the list you applied"],
  "formatting_matched": ["which formatting habits you matched"],
  "confidence": 0.85,
  "self_critique": "what feels off, if anything — be honest, not optimistic"
}}
```

traces.prompt:
You are a structural analyst specializing in viral content mechanics. Dissect this viral post into its mechanical parts — structure, not content. The move is what carries over. The source author's typography, lowercase, emojis, register — those are their voice, not the hook's engine.

## SOURCE VIRAL POST
Sales leaders: After working with 5,000 revenue orgs, I've seen 5 patterns in every great sales team.

From InsideSales, to Gong, to pclub.io – my career has been in the walls of revenue teams.

5 things the best do:


1. They know where they win.

They don’t chase the market.

They chase the segment where they have unfair advantage.

They define a surgical ICP and stop wasting cycles on deals that never close.

They’re obsessed with:

• Where they win
• Where they lose
• Where win-rate is too low

Then they operationalize it.

They don’t just "know" where they win.

They run the business around it.

One CRO I talked to said this:

“If you want higher close rates, stop chasing bad deals.”


2. They’re obsessed with narrative.

Once they know the territory, they design the narrative that unlocks it.

They refine messaging until buyers think:

“They understand my world better than I do.”

Narrative isn’t a marketing exercise.

It’s fuel that drives revenue.

When you nail it, everything is easier.

Whether it’s the CMO, CRO, or even CEO, someone holds this job:

“Chief Narrative Officer.”


3. They build a performance culture.

The best sales teams take a page from Netflix:

“We’re not a family. We’re a pro sports team.”

• Camaraderie? Yes.
• Psychological safety? Yes.

But also:

We’re here to perform.

If someone isn’t pulling their weight, the culture addresses it.

Elite teams balance two forces:

A) High standards
B) High safety

The paradox:

The more transparent you are about:

• Performance expectations
• PIP criteria

…the less fear exists.

Performance expectations create short-term fear.

But ambiguity creates permanent fear.

Open expectations remove "wondering."

Reps know where they stand.

That frees them.


4. They build rock-solid stages & exit criteria.

Great teams don’t use vague stages like Discovery → Demo → Proposal.

They design a sales process that exposes the reality of a deal.

• Clear stage definition
• Binary exit criteria
• Aging discipline

This clarity drives predictability:

• Reps stop guessing
• Managers coach w/precision
• Forecasts stop lying

Process definition is the compass.

But here’s the trap:

Having a clean process still isn't enough for consistency.

Sales stages and exit criteria only define what to do.

They do not equip reps with how to do it.


5. They treat skills like a performance system.

Strong leaders don’t just tell reps what to do.

They build the skill capacity to do it.

Once you define a great process, a hard truth emerges:

Many reps don’t have enough skill capacity to do it.

Great teams systematize skill excellence.

They treat skill capacity like a monetizeable asset.

These teams don’t view skills as “our people should already have these.”

They design skill profiles, measure them, train them.

Process without skill is academically strong, commercially weak.

Skill without process is chaos.

Do both?

You unlock revenue excellence.

Which of these 5 stood out most?

## PLATFORM
linkedin

---

## TASK: Dissect the Hook

### 1. THE PSYCHOLOGICAL MOVE
Figure out what the hook does to the reader — the psychological move. Examples:
- Three-stack pain escalation (old pain + new pain + meta-trap for objecting)
- Second-party verdict that reframes the speaker's whole career
- Contrarian claim that inverts conventional wisdom
- Specific number or age anchor that signals real memory
- Authority figure delivering a quote the reader doesn't expect
- Parallel data points with a third-line reveal
- Scene drop that puts the reader inside a moment mid-action
- Diagnostic question that makes the reader self-categorize
- Confession that costs the speaker credibility

The move is what carries over to the generated posts. Everything else is surface.

### 2. NARRATIVE ARC
What movement does this post run on? (contradiction reveal, escalation, scene-to-insight, confession-to-principle, diagnostic revelation, progressive build)

### 3. HOOK MECHANICS — SENTENCE-LEVEL BREAKDOWN
For each opening sentence (typically 1-3 lines before the body begins):
- The exact text
- What this sentence DOES structurally (permission frame, diagnostic question, consequence statement, reframe, scene drop, specificity bomb, tension gap)
- Its rhythm (short/punchy, medium/flowing, long/building)
- The psychological effect: WHY does it stop the scroll?

### 4. SENTENCE MAP
Exact sentence count and structural function of opening lines. This is the mold for Batch A mirroring.

### 5. BODY STRUCTURE
How is the body organized? (single scene explored deeply, three examples building, before/after transformation, progressive revelation, numbered list with twist)

### 6. ENDING TYPE
(concrete image, challenge to reader, quiet admission, unresolved tension, physical detail, callback to opening)

### 7. MIRRORABILITY ASSESSMENT
Can this source's opening be mirrored (same shape, new content)?
- **Mirrorable**: the sentence shape works with different domain content. The hook's engine is structural, not content-dependent.
- **Not mirrorable**: the hook is too proper-noun-specific, too culturally embedded, or too short to mirror without plagiarism risk. If not mirrorable, flag it and all 9 posts should be Batch B.

### 8. REGISTER NOTE
If the source's register is doing mechanical work — rant energy, whispered vulnerability, exasperation — note it. This energy must be translated into the founder's register, not stripped.

### 9. VIRALITY REASON
One sentence: what makes the opening work for a general audience?

### 10. OPENER QUALITY TESTS
Run these three tests on the source's opening line(s). These determine whether the opener is good enough to mirror (Batch A) or whether all posts should use different mechanics (Batch B only).

**MACRO test**: Does the opener land for someone OUTSIDE this niche? Read the opener to a stranger in a different industry. If they'd need domain context to understand what's being said — fail. Jargon, acronyms, category-specific terms ("AI-powered voice technology," "doctorless AI kiosks," "accessible communication") → fail. The opener must work universally.

**FAST test**: Does the specific, interesting, or dramatic word land in the FIRST 8 words? Count from word 1. If the payload (the specific number, the surprising claim, the tension) comes after word 8 — fail. Ellipsis that delays the punch ("The future of X is arriving...") → fail. "We lost $2M in three months" → pass (payload at word 3). "The future of healthcare is arriving... China unveils..." → fail (payload at word 11).

**SIMPLE test**: Would a 16-year-old understand every word in the opener? Bold Unicode formatting, corporate jargon ("unveils," "redefining," "accessible communication"), press-release language → fail. The opener must use words a person would speak in conversation, not words a company would put in a press release.

**Rule**: If ANY of the three tests fails, set `skip_batch_a` to true. The source's STRUCTURE may be mirrorable, but if its OPENER is a press release, corporate announcement, or jargon-heavy statement, mirroring it produces a press-release post. Route all posts to Batch B instead, where different mechanics will produce better openers.

---

## OUTPUT FORMAT

```json
{{
  "psychological_move": "description of the core psychological move the hook makes",
  "narrative_arc": "description of movement/arc",
  "hook_mechanic_primary": "three_stack_pain|second_party_verdict|contrarian_claim|specific_number|authority_quote|parallel_data_reveal|scene_entry|diagnostic_question|confession|pattern_observation",
  "opener_mechanic_description": "concise description of HOW the mechanic operates in this specific opener — structural moves in order, e.g. 'authority + count: audience address → credential from scale → numbered promise' or 'scene entry: mid-action drop → sensory detail → tension gap'",
  "source_opener_text": "the full opener text — all hook sentences joined with newlines exactly as written",
  "hook_mechanics": [
    {{
      "sentence": "exact text of opening sentence",
      "structural_function": "what this sentence does",
      "psychological_effect": "why it stops the scroll",
      "rhythm": "short|medium|long"
    }}
  ],
  "sentence_count": 2,
  "body_structure": "description of body organization",
  "ending_type": "description of ending mechanic",
  "mirrorable": true,
  "mirrorability_reason": "why or why not — be specific",
  "register_energy": "the emotional register doing mechanical work, if any",
  "virality_reason": "why the opening works for a general audience",
  "opener_tests": {{
    "macro": {{"pass": true, "reason": "why it passes or fails for a general audience"}},
    "fast": {{"pass": true, "first_payload_word": 3, "reason": "where the payload lands"}},
    "simple": {{"pass": true, "reason": "whether a 16-year-old would understand every word"}}
  }},
  "skip_batch_a": false,
  "named_entities": ["list of proper names (people) mentioned in the source post"]
}}
```

Focus on MECHANICS, not meaning. The content changes; the machine stays the same.

The best sales teams take a page from Netflix:

We’re here to perform.

B) High safety

The more transparent you are about:

Reps know where they stand.

They build the skill capacity to do it.

They design skill profiles, measure them, train them.

Skill without process is chaos.

Do both?

You unlock revenue excellence.

You're building a waiting room.')
- Never says 'AI is replacing humans' — always reframes as 'AI exposes who was actually adding value'
- Uses 'quietly' as a signal word for hidden damage — ('quietly defunded three years ago,' 'quietly blow up a company')
- Defines by negation — tells you what something is NOT before telling you what it IS ('High retention doesn't mean you built something great, it means...')
- Uses 'compounds' and compounding language as a recurring evaluative frame — trust compounds, intensity compounds, waiting compounds, antibodies compound
- Ends posts with a period, never a question. Never asks 'What do you think?' or 'Agree?' — the closing is always a statement that lands like a verdict

## POST MODE (wrestling/declaring)
declaring

---

## 7-GATE DIAGNOSIS

Run the gates in order. Each gate is cheap to run on a draft that already passed the previous one.

### Gate 1: MECHANIC (does the opener stop the scroll?)
Identify which of the 8 proven mechanics is being used:
1. Specific number + context — "$412K closed last quarter. Target was $350K."
2. Juxtaposition — "Our competitor raised $40M. We outshipped them."
3. First-person confession — "I told 50 buyers we were ready. I was wrong."
4. Quote hook + reaction — "'We need you to present to the supervisor.' That's when I knew the deal was dead."
5. Scene-entry — "We walked into their call center. Post-it notes on every monitor."
6. Decision reveal — "A Fortune 500 asked us to build a CRM. We said no."
7. Contrarian declaration — "Most founder advice is wrong."
8. Pattern observation — "Every founder I know who's crossed $20M runs into the same wall."

FAIL: Belief statements ("X matters"), question openers, "excited to announce," hedged setups, "I've been thinking about...", "What's your X strategy?"

### Gate 2: PLAUSIBILITY (would a real person actually say this in real life?)
Run this test on every opener containing a quote, scene, or stated dialogue. Specific failure shapes to catch:

- **Strawman quote**: A character delivering a too-convenient line that exists only so the post can knock it down. Real executives don't say these teed-up sentences at dinner.
- **Counted-precision tic**: "She has been in eleven AI strategy meetings since January. Eleven." Real people don't volunteer specific meeting counts. The repetition for emphasis is a written rhetorical move, not spoken.
- **Convenient setup quote**: Someone brags about something then can't answer the obvious follow-up. The exchange exists for the punchline.
- **The obvious-question quote from someone who wouldn't ask it**: Board members at Series B AI companies ask about pipeline, churn, runway — not "when does AI just handle everything?"
- **The impossible scenario**: The setup is logically impossible given the rest of the post.

If the opener has a quote or scene with another person, ask: would a real person in that role, in that moment, actually say or do this? If "no, this is set up too neatly to be real" — FAIL.

### Gate 3: COHERENCE (does the opener match the post's actual argument?)
The single most important test. An opener can be punchy AND wrong for the post. Two sub-tests, both required:

**Promise test**: Write down what the opener promises the reader, in one sentence. Read the body. Does every paragraph deliver against that promise, or does the body argue an adjacent point?

**Compression test**: Compress the body to one sentence. Compare to the opener. Same argument from different angles, or two different arguments?

Failure modes:
- **Drift** — opener is right, body wandered. Fix the body.
- **Bait-and-switch** — opener is sharper than the body delivers. Match one to the other.
- **Two posts in one** — opener for argument A, body for argument B. Split.

The opener has to be both universal (simple words, stakes a stranger gets) AND faithful to the actual argument. Macro-but-misleading damages the founder over time.

### Gate 4: SPECIFICITY (no invented facts)
Three degrees of specificity:
- **First-degree**: "Anant told me on the flight to Manila…" — Allowed ONLY if it's in the draft or the founder has publicly told this story.
- **Second-degree**: "A founder I back at a $40M company told me…" — Allowed only if the founder really has companies at that scale.
- **Third-degree**: "Every founder I know who's crossed $20M runs into the same thing…" — Always allowed.

Default to third-degree when in doubt. A pattern observation lands almost as hard as a named story and carries zero fabrication risk. Never invent first-degree.

### Gate 5: MODE PRESERVATION
The post arrives already marked as wrestling-mode or declaring-mode.

**Declaring-mode**: strongest opener is usually a clean assertion, contrarian declaration, or specific number. Body defends the assertion in a straight line.

**Wrestling-mode**: strongest opener signals movement is coming — a confession ("I told 50 buyers we were ready. I was wrong"), a contradiction ("Our biggest churn last quarter wasn't who I expected"), or a stated tension.

A wrestling-mode post with a clean declarative opener creates a bait-and-switch: reader is promised certainty, body delivers uncertainty. Don't silently strip wrestling structure to make an opener punchier. The wrestling IS the point.

### Gate 6: VOICE FIT (does this opener sound like THIS founder?)
Compare the opener against the founder's voice markers.

Two sub-tests:

1. **Recognition test**: Would this founder read the opener and think "this is what I was going to say"? Or would they think "this is what my marketing team wrote"? If the opener could appear in a corporate press release, product announcement, or generic LinkedIn post — FAIL. Test: strip the name and ask "who wrote this?" If the answer is "any executive" rather than "this specific founder" — FAIL.

2. **Marker test**: Does the opener contain or reflect at least 1 of the founder's voice markers? Not forced inclusion — natural expression. If the opener uses corporate register (e.g., "unveils," "AI-powered," "accessible communication," "the future of X is arriving," "redefining") instead of the founder's register — FAIL.

The voice markers are the law. If the opener is mechanically perfect (passes Gates 1-5) but sounds like someone else wrote it — FAIL Gate 6. A technically correct opener that doesn't sound like the founder is worse than a rough opener that does.

### Gate 7: SOURCE MIRROR FIDELITY (Batch A only)
Skip this gate entirely if source dissection is "N/A" (Batch B posts). Only Batch A mirrored posts are tested here.

Source primary mechanic: unknown
Source dissection: {'narrative_arc': 'unknown', 'mirrorable': True}

Four sub-tests:
1. **Mechanic match**: Does the opener use the SAME primary mechanic as the source (unknown)? If the opener substitutes a different mechanic (e.g., source uses "specific_number" but opener uses "contrarian") — FAIL. This is the most critical sub-test.
2. **Sentence count match**: Does the opener have the same number of sentences as the source's hook?
3. **Word count proximity**: Is the opener's word count within ±20% of the source opener's word count?
4. **Structural beat match**: Does each opener sentence serve the same structural function as the corresponding source sentence?

If the mechanic doesn't match (sub-test 1), this gate FAILS regardless of other sub-tests. The entire point of Batch A is mechanic mirroring — a Batch A post that uses a different mechanic has failed its core purpose.

## BODY SCAN
Read the body line by line. For each line, ask:
- Could this sentence work as the opener, lifted out of where it currently sits?
- Does it have a number, a juxtaposition, a confession, a quote, or a scene?
- Is it punchier than what's currently in line 1?

If yes, mark it as buried gold. Don't draft the new opener until you've checked whether the post already contains its real opener three paragraphs in.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS
- Opens with a paradoxical declarative statement — no preamble, no 'I've been thinking about...' Just the claim. ('Everyone is wrong about which incumbents AI will kill.' / 'The worst operators I know can name exactly who the CEO trusts.')
- Uses 'you're not X, you're Y' reframe structure — ('You're not cutting costs, you're cutting the pipeline that produces your next decade of leadership.' / 'You're not getting better at serving the market, you're getting better at serving the people who already fit.')
- Second-person 'you' as implication device — makes the reader feel they might be the one making the mistake, without accusation
- Em dashes for mid-sentence pivots and parenthetical precision — used heavily, 2-4 per post, never semicolons
- Closing with a conditional reframe: 'If your [X] isn't [Y], you're [Z]' — ('If your retention work isn't changing who you acquire, you're optimizing the wrong layer.' / 'If your trust surface is under 10 people in a company over 200, you're not building an organization. You're building a waiting room.')
- Never says 'AI is replacing humans' — always reframes as 'AI exposes who was actually adding value'
- Uses 'quietly' as a signal word for hidden damage — ('quietly defunded three years ago,' 'quietly blow up a company')
- Defines by negation — tells you what something is NOT before telling you what it IS ('High retention doesn't mean you built something great, it means...')
- Uses 'compounds' and compounding language as a recurring evaluative frame — trust compounds, intensity compounds, waiting compounds, antibodies compound
- Ends posts with a period, never a question. Never asks 'What do you think?' or 'Agree?' — the closing is always a statement that lands like a verdict

## POST MODE: declaring

## THE 13 PROVEN MECHANICS
Each version must use a different one. Pick the 5 best fits for this post:
1. Specific number + context — lead with a precise figure from the draft. "$412K closed last quarter. Target was $350K."
2. Juxtaposition — two things that shouldn't coexist in the same sentence. "Our competitor raised $40M. We outshipped them with 3 engineers."
3. First-person confession — admit something with real cost. "I told 50 buyers we were ready. I was wrong."
4. Quote hook + reaction — someone said something, then your reaction. "'We need you to present to the supervisor.' That's when I knew the deal was dead."
5. Scene-entry — start in the middle of a physical moment. "We walked into their call center. Post-it notes on every monitor."
6. Decision reveal — the outcome of a hard choice, stated plainly. "A Fortune 500 asked us to build a CRM. We said no."
7. Contrarian declaration — state something most would disagree with (earned, not clickbait). "Most founder advice is wrong."
8. Pattern observation — notice something others miss. "Every founder I know who's crossed $20M runs into the same wall."
9. Tension pair — name two forces pulling in opposite directions. "The market says automate everything. Customers keep asking for humans."
10. Before/after snapshot — two states separated by time, the contrast implies the story. "Two years ago we couldn't close a single deal. Last month we turned down three."
11. Micro story — a complete narrative arc in 1-2 sentences. "An investor asked what happens if a competitor copies us. I showed him our NPS scores."
12. Authority redirect — cite a credible external authority, then redirect their point. "McKinsey says 70% of transformations fail. They left out why the 30% succeed."
13. Data inversion — take a commonly cited stat and show it means the opposite. "95% customer satisfaction. We were measuring the wrong customers."

- Multiple exclamation marks → one or zero
- Stat-stack fragments → a sentence containing the stat
